title: Leader-follower response features from local rival state, not whole-field averages
summary: Recent F1 strategy work models pit timing as a competitive dynamic program or Stackelberg game, implying that immediate rival state matters more than broad field averages.
rationale: Recent F1 strategy work models pit timing as a competitive dynamic program or Stackelberg game, implying that immediate rival state matters more than broad field averages. That suggests a narrower expert than the prior broad team/undercut ideas: features describing whether the driver is defending against the car behind or attacking the car ahead right now.
implementation_hint: For each row, build causal features using only current and previous laps: nearest car ahead/behind on same lap by position, their compound/stint/tyre life, local tyre-age differential, whether the driver is leader/follower in the local pair, and a simple 'cover vs attack' proxy such as `own_tyre_life - rival_tyre_life`, interacted with position and race phase. Train a small booster on these local-rival features alone, then blend as a specialist.
expected_effect: Should improve hard strategic cases where pit decisions are driven by one nearby competitor rather than absolute degradation level.
risk: Position is only ordinal and not time-gap, so local-rival proxies may be noisy; if engineered too aggressively this can collapse into weak heuristics.
